old_claim.note: Printed Map Atlases provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The Depot produced and conserved military maps and had an explicit topographic/cartographic function, so cartographic practice is the enabling capability rather than bound printed atlases.

source_shape.source_locator: Ministere de la Culture glossary entry: created in 1688 by Louvois, the Depot de la Guerre produced and conserved military maps and had an important topographic and cartographic function.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies the institution as a military-map bureau with cartographic and topographic functions.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports replacing printed atlases because the source-backed capability is making and preserving military maps, not the narrower artifact category of bound atlases.

ontology_before: Military Map Rooms depended directly on Printed Map Atlases, laundering a publication format into a broader military cartography/archive office.
ontology_after: Depot de la Guerre Military Map Archive depends directly on cartography as the map-making and topographic practice that the source describes.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: military_map_rooms no longer depends on printed_map_atlases and now depends on cartography.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped back to bound printed military atlases rather than the Depot bureau.
A source showed the 1688 Depot required printed atlases specifically, not cartography and map production more broadly.
The cartography node were narrowed away from map-making and topographic representation.
short_reason: Cartography is the source-backed map-production capability; printed atlases are too narrow.
